data.title: 2025 Annual Report on Anti-Money Laundering Compliance of Virtual Assets
data.description:
Foreword

2025 is a pivotal year for establishing the global regulatory landscape for virtual assets, and also a year in which anti-money laundering compliance faces significant challenges. From the implementation of the US GENIUS Act and the full implementation of the EU MiCA, to the passage of the Hong Kong Stablecoin Ordinance and the advancement of the ASPIRe roadmap, major jurisdictions worldwide are accelerating the construction of systematic and multi-dimensional regulatory systems. At the same time, virtual asset crimes are exhibiting cross-border, organized, and technological characteristics, posing a severe challenge to traditional anti-money laundering systems.

This report, initiated by Beosin and co-authored with the Association of Certified Anti-Money Laundering Professionals (ACAMS) and the Association of Blockchain Compliance Professionals (ABCP), focuses on the global anti-money laundering and regulatory dynamics of virtual assets in 2025. It systematically reviews the regulatory landscape of major countries and regions, deeply analyzes the characteristics of virtual asset crimes and money laundering methods, and focuses on emerging technology trends such as the x402 payment protocol, dedicated chains for stablecoins, and prediction markets. It aims to present an in-depth and valuable annual observation for industry practitioners, regulatory agencies, and researchers from an objective and professional perspective. 1. Analysis of the Global Anti-Money Laundering and Regulatory Landscape of Virtual Assets 2025 is considered a watershed year in the history of global virtual asset (Virtual Assets) regulation. If the previous decade was a period of observation and trial and error for regulatory agencies worldwide regarding this emerging asset class, then 2025 marks the formal establishment and systematization of the global regulatory framework. With blockchain technology deeply embedded in global financial infrastructure, governments no longer examine virtual assets solely from the perspective of anti-money laundering (AML) and counter-terrorism financing (CFT), but rather view them as a core component of the future digital economy, thereby constructing a comprehensive governance system encompassing prudent regulation, market behavior, investor protection, and systemic risk prevention. This year's regulatory focus exhibits a profound paradigm shift: regulators have moved from passively responding to market innovation to proactively setting technical standards and market access rules through legislation. This is particularly evident in the enactment of the US GENIUS Act, the full implementation of the EU's Crypto Asset Market Regulation Act (MiCA), and the passage of Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance and the advancement of the "ASPIRe" strategic roadmap. Major global financial centers have essentially completed the closed loop of their top-level design. This trend from "vague exploration" to "established rules" not only clears legal obstacles for the large-scale entry of traditional financial institutions (TradFi), but also poses unprecedented challenges to the compliant survival capabilities of native crypto companies. At a deeper level, the tightening and clarification of regulations actually paves the way for the "decriminalization" and "financial instrumentation" of virtual assets, which is positive for the long-term healthy development of the industry. With the improvement of the regulatory framework, regulatory arbitrage opportunities are effectively compressed, which further separates illicit funds from the compliant market, prompting the overall ecosystem to develop in a more standardized and transparent direction. The increasingly clear boundary between the compliant market and illegal activities not only enhances the confidence of market participants but also lays the foundation for the integration of virtual assets into the mainstream financial system. 1.1 Regulatory Landscape of Virtual Assets in Major Countries and Regions in 2025 1.1.1 United States Regulatory Landscape: In July 2025, the United States formally passed the GENIUS Act, establishing a regulatory framework for payment-type stablecoins. This marked the first time the United States legislated to recognize and regulate stablecoins at the federal level: ● Clearly defines stablecoins as neither securities nor commodities; ● Issuers must hold a 1:1 reserve (cash or US Treasury bonds within 93 days); ● Establishes a "Stablecoin Certification Review Committee" and restricts access to technology giants (such as...) Meta and Apple issue stablecoins; ● Strengthen KYC, AML, and consumer protection; ● Foreign issuers (such as Tether) must register in the US, otherwise they will be prohibited from entering the market. Learning from the painful lessons of the Terra/Luna crash and FTX's misappropriation of user assets, the GENIUS Act sets almost stringent standards for the management of reserve assets. The Act mandates that issuers hold a 1:1 reserve asset ratio, and eligible assets are limited to cash or US Treasury bills with a maturity of 93 days or less. This provision excludes high-risk assets such as commercial paper and corporate bonds, ensuring the high liquidity and security of the underlying assets of stablecoins. A more crucial institutional innovation lies in bankruptcy remoteness and priority of repayment rights. The bill modifies the application logic of bankruptcy law, explicitly stipulating that stablecoin holders have a "first priority" over other general unsecured creditors of the issuer when the issuer goes bankrupt or becomes insolvent. This provision fundamentally addresses the risk of user funds becoming "unsecured claims," ​​greatly enhancing institutional investors' confidence in using stablecoins for large-scale settlements. This legal certainty is a prerequisite for traditional financial institutions to incorporate stablecoins into their core business and lays a legal foundation of trust for emerging technologies such as the "x402 payment protocol" mentioned in later chapters. Currently, the US virtual asset industry is regulated collaboratively by multiple agencies: the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates virtual currencies with securities attributes; the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) regulates virtual currency futures contract trading; the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) regulates stablecoin issuance, custody, and payment; and the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) combats money laundering and other illegal activities. While establishing stringent standards, regulators are also loosening restrictions on traditional financial institutions. The Federal Reserve has lifted previous restrictions on banks engaging in crypto business, allowing them to decide independently whether to offer custody, payment, and issuance services for crypto assets, provided they have established a sound risk management system. In December 2025, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) further approved a proposal to implement the application process of the GENIUS Act, clarifying the specific procedures for bank subsidiaries to apply for the issuance of stablecoins. However, due to concerns about antitrust and data privacy, the Act established a "Stablecoin Certification Review Board" and imposed additional restrictions on large technology companies (such as Meta and Apple) issuing stablecoins. This differentiated treatment reflects regulators' wariness of the "commercial data + financial monopoly" model, aiming to prevent tech giants from using their vast user networks to form a closed-loop ecosystem of supranational currencies. 1.1.2 Hong Kong Regulatory Landscape: In February 2025, the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) released the highly forward-looking "ASPIRe" regulatory roadmap, aiming to comprehensively reshape Hong Kong's virtual asset financial ecosystem through five pillars. ● Access: Simplifying market access through regulatory clarity. The focus in 2025 is on filling regulatory gaps in over-the-counter (OTC) trading and custody services, establishing a licensing system to allow compliant institutions to enter the market more smoothly, while excluding illegal OTC trading from the formal financial system. ● Safeguards: Optimizing compliance burdens. Regulators acknowledge that past "one-size-fits-all" rules (such as extremely high cold wallet storage requirements) may have hampered operational efficiency, and are therefore shifting towards a "risk-based" regulatory approach, exploring adjustments to custody technology standards and storage ratios based on technological advancements. ● Products: Enriching product offerings. The SFC has explicitly stated that it will expand the range of tradable assets and services based on investor categories (retail vs. professional), breaking away from the previous limitation to only a few mainstream cryptocurrencies. ● Infrastructure: Modernizing regulatory infrastructure. Utilizing regulatory technology (RegTech) to improve reporting and monitoring efficiency, and strengthening cross-departmental intelligence sharing among the SFC, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), and the police force to address increasingly sophisticated on-chain crime. ● Relationships: Strengthening ecosystem connections. Strengthen cooperation with global regulatory agencies, promote cross-border law enforcement cooperation, and rebuild market confidence through investor education. In May 2025, the Hong Kong Legislative Council passed the Stablecoin Ordinance, which officially came into effect in August 2025. This makes Hong Kong one of the few jurisdictions in Asia to have implemented institutionalized regulation of stablecoins. *Details on the Stablecoin Ordinance can be found in the full version. Regarding the issue exposed in the JPEX case—unlicensed OTC entities (such as street money changers) becoming entry points for fraudulent funds—the Hong Kong government completed consultations on the OTC and custodian licensing system in June 2025 and initiated the legislative process in the second half of the year. ● OTC Regulation: The SFC is responsible for this, focusing on combating money laundering risks and requiring OTC merchants to conduct strict customer registration and transaction limit management. This move directly targets the cash laundering methods employed by "black and gray market guarantee platforms" using offline OTC transactions, as described in Chapter Two of this report. ● Custody Regulation: The SFC will establish dedicated custody licenses (Type 13 RA), bringing independent custodians into the regulatory system. This ends the previous situation where only VATP subsidiaries could provide compliant custody services, enabling professional third-party custody institutions (such as banks and trust companies) to obtain independent licenses and provide safer asset custody services to the market. The improved regulatory system for virtual assets provides solid support for law enforcement practices. In August 2024, the Organized Crime and Triad Bureau (OCTB) of the Hong Kong Police Force issued a formal letter of appreciation to a cryptocurrency exchange, recognizing the exchange's investigation team's contribution to a major kidnapping case. The letter of appreciation was personally signed by OCTB Senior Superintendent Au Yeung Chiu-kong, who specifically thanked one investigator from the exchange's investigation team, who recently joined the ABCP as a member of the Executive Committee. In the letter, the Hong Kong Police Force thanked the investigators for their assistance in successfully identifying suspects within a criminal syndicate, thereby advancing the case investigation. The exchange, through intelligence analysis by its Financial Crime Compliance Team (FCC), provided crucial leads to help the police track down the suspects. This case demonstrates that criminals may use cryptocurrency as a tool for transferring funds in various types of criminal activities, such as ransom laundering after kidnapping or cross-border money flows. However, leveraging expertise in blockchain analytics and professional investigative techniques, private analytics firms and law enforcement agencies were able to effectively track on-chain fund flows, identify anomalous patterns, and pinpoint suspects, significantly improving investigative efficiency. This letter of appreciation not only highlights the effectiveness of public-private partnerships in combating emerging technology crimes but also serves as a positive example of how blockchain analytics and intelligence firms like Beosin and ABCP, as well as compliance organizations, can promote interaction between the industry and law enforcement agencies, proving that professional blockchain investigative capabilities can play a crucial role in diverse criminal scenarios. 1.1.3 EU Regulatory Landscape: Although some provisions of the Crypto Asset Markets Act (MiCA) came into effect in 2024, it wasn't until December 30, 2024, that the Act fully covered all 27 EU member states and 3 EEA countries, achieving unified regulatory rules. MiCA divides stablecoins into Electronic Money Tokens (EMTs) and Asset Reference Tokens (ARTs). EMTs maintain their value by referencing an official currency (i.e., fiat-backed stablecoins), while ARTs stabilize their value by referencing a combination of values ​​or rights. MiCA stipulates that issuers of EMTs must be EU-authorized Credit Institutions (i.e., banks) or Electronic Money Institutions (EMIs). This means that issuers without an EMI license in the EU cannot list their tokens on exchanges within the EU. This regulation dealt a devastating blow to Tether (USDT). Because Tether failed to apply for or obtain an EU license, major exchanges such as Coinbase, OKX, and Bitstamp announced the delisting of USDT for European users in late 2024 and early 2025. ● Stablecoin issuers are required to publish white papers, disclosing detailed information about the issuer, stablecoin characteristics, attached rights and obligations, underlying technology, risk factors, and asset reserves. ● Any marketing and communications related to stablecoin trading must be clear and unambiguous, avoiding inflammatory statements to control speculation risks at the source. ● Issuers must ensure 100% underlying asset reserves and report regularly to guarantee stablecoin value. ● The daily trading volume of a single ART or EMT must not exceed €5 million. When the market value of ART or EMT exceeds €500 million, the issuer must report to the regulatory authorities and take additional compliance measures. ● When the usage of ART exceeds 1 million transactions or the transaction amount reaches 200 million euros (quarterly average) within a single currency area, the issuance of that ART must be stopped. ● Only euro stablecoins can be used for everyday payments for goods and services, limiting the proportion of non-euro stablecoins in the EU. **According to Decta's "2025 Euro Stablecoin Trends Report," within 12 months of the implementation of MiCA, the market capitalization of major euro stablecoins increased by 102%, completely reversing the downward trend of the previous year: **● EURC (Circle): As a euro stablecoin issued by Circle, its transaction volume increased by an astonishing 1139% after the implementation of MiCA, and it holds more than 50% of the market share in circulation, becoming the de facto leader in euro stablecoins. ●EURS (Stasis): As a long-established euro stablecoin, its market capitalization surged from $38 million to $283 million, a growth rate exceeding 600%. ●EURCV (Société Générale - Forge): Issued by a subsidiary of Société Générale, EURCV represents a traditional bank's attempt to enter this field. Its trading volume increased by 343%, and although the base is small, it is symbolically significant—bank-issued stablecoins are beginning to gain liquidity in the DeFi space. Furthermore, the Digital Operations Resilience Act (DORA) officially came into effect on January 17, 2025. While DORA is not solely aimed at the crypto industry but covers all financial institutions, its impact on crypto businesses is particularly profound.

● Core Requirements: DORA requires financial entities to establish extremely high-standard IT security systems to defend against cyberattacks and technical failures. It grants regulators the power to directly scrutinize key ICT third-party service providers (such as cloud service providers and node service providers).

● Industry Impact: This means that crypto exchanges and custodians must not only manage their own systems but also be responsible for the stability of the on-chain infrastructure they rely on (such as oracles and node networks). This echoes the "prediction market oracle manipulation risk" mentioned in Chapter 4 of this report—regulators are beginning to focus on the single point of failure risk of decentralized infrastructure.

Furthermore, the newly established European Anti-Money Laundering Authority (AMLA), which will begin operations in 2025, will directly regulate high-risk CASPs, with a particular emphasis on monitoring cross-border transactions and privacy coins, consistent with the global trend of combating crypto money laundering.

*For more information on regulatory trends in other countries and regions, please refer to the full annual report.* II. Global Virtual Asset Crime Landscape In 2025, the rapid expansion of the virtual currency ecosystem and technological innovation went hand in hand, leading to a comprehensive upgrade in criminal methods. From on-chain attacks and cross-chain money laundering to generative AI fraud scenarios and dark web quick payment scenarios, virtual currency crimes have become more prominent in terms of concealment, intelligence, and cross-border operations. Transnational criminal groups have rapidly expanded in terms of organizational scale, business chains, and modus operandi, and their cross-border collaboration and risk spillover capabilities have shown unprecedented expansion. From early-stage targeted fraud and mid-stage technical attacks to later-stage laundering of illicit funds, each stage is handled by professional teams. They build cross-border collaborative organizational systems through encrypted communication tools and offshore company networks, forming a closed-loop model of "headquarters command + regional execution + global money laundering." The February 2025 hack of the Bybit exchange became a landmark event in large-scale transnational criminal activities. Furthermore, the Chen Zhi case of the Southeast Asian Prince Group, exposed in the fourth quarter, further revealed the "industry coverage + global money laundering" characteristics of such criminal networks. This "corporate criminal ecosystem" poses a significant challenge to traditional judicial cooperation and regulatory mechanisms in terms of response speed and technological capabilities. 2.1 Guarantee Platforms Become a Major Channel for Collusion Between Black and Gray Industries In black and gray industry transaction scenarios, the lack of mutual trust between the transacting parties is one of the core pain points. This problem directly gave rise to the role of "guarantee" as an intermediary. Such guarantee platforms are essentially transaction models involving third-party guarantors (including individuals, intelligent programs, or service institutions). Their core function is to assume intermediary guarantee responsibilities throughout the entire transaction process, establishing trust and endorsement between the transacting parties, thereby promoting cooperation. As a new type of platform facilitating black and gray market activities, escrow platforms have experienced exponential growth in both user base and capital volume in recent years. Beosin statistics show that as of December 15, 2025, mainstream escrow platforms had over 549,400 users who had deposited funds over the years, with over 2.54 million deposits and a total deposited capital flow exceeding 15 billion USDT. In 2025 alone, mainstream escrow platforms had over 330,000 users who had deposited funds, over 1.26 million deposits, and a deposited capital flow exceeding 8.7 billion USDT. Currently, escrow platforms primarily operate through anonymous chat software (such as Telegram), providing one-stop, comprehensive services to black and gray market activities by creating groups and related channels. In 2025, the United States intensified its sanctions against cybercrime in Southeast Asia. In May, the United States announced sanctions against Funnull Technology Inc. and its executives in the Philippines for involvement in virtual asset scams; in October, sanctions were imposed on the Prince Group in Cambodia and 146 related individuals, freezing and seizing 127,271 bitcoins (worth approximately $15 billion at the time), and formally severing the connection between Huiwang Group and the US financial system; in November, sanctions were imposed on organizations in Myanmar involved in online fraud. This series of sanctions forced Southeast Asian criminal organizations, represented by Huiwang Guarantee, to cease operations or relocate. 2.1.1 Huiwang Guarantee Among guarantee platforms, the most representative is "Huiwang Guarantee" (renamed "Haowang Guarantee" in October 2024 to evade regulatory scrutiny), which was formerly affiliated with Huiwang Group. Huiwang Group, formerly known as Huiwang Currency Exchange (Cambodia) Co., Ltd., claims to have been established in August 2014 and is a large financial group with multiple financial licenses, including payment, banking, and insurance. Backed by Huiwang Group, Huiwang Guarantee has rapidly developed into the largest guarantee platform on Telegram, employing a three-tiered structure of "General Group - Regional Groups - Business Groups." The General Group is controlled by 10 core administrators, and the Regional Groups are divided according to business type (including "Entertainment Funds Guarantee Group," "Payment Exchange Guarantee Group," etc.). The Business Groups are private groups for one-on-one client interaction. In addition to Telegram, the guarantee platform also receives large order inquiries through chat software such as WhatsApp and Tudou, requiring settlements to be made using virtual currencies such as USDT and TRX, refusing direct fiat currency transactions. As of May 2025, Huiwang Guarantee had 9,289 active public groups on Telegram, each with approximately 1,000-5,000 members, and was linked to 12,000 private business groups, of which "entertainment funding matching groups" accounted for 23%. In mid-May 2025, Huiwang Guarantee was banned from Telegram, and the platform announced its cessation of operations. The direct reason for the Telegram ban was likely US sanctions. On May 1, 2025, the US Treasury Department's Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) announced that it had designated Huiwang Group as a "major foreign financial institution of money laundering concern" and intended to add it to the "311" list. Being added to the "311" list would cut off access to the US dollar settlement system, meaning Huiwang Group would be unable to complete US dollar transactions through the US financial system. Furthermore, exchanges and wallet service providers, as well as other blockchain operators, would also be unable to provide services to it. 2.1.2 Tudou Guarantee Huiwang Guarantee's business was suspended due to the banning of its core Telegram operating group, and it urgently needed external connections to maintain its operations. To quickly resume operations, Huiwang Guarantee adopted a dual-binding strategy of "equity linkage + business takeover": ● Equity Linkage Huiwang Guarantee directly acquired 30% of Tudou Guarantee's shares, directly accessing Tudou Guarantee through shareholding. This avoided the regulatory sensitivity caused by directly restarting the original brand, while ensuring the continued development of its guarantee business. ● Business Succession Following the equity acquisition, Huiwang Guarantee immediately guided users from the original platform to Tudou Guarantee and other affiliated platforms, essentially continuing the original business logic of Huiwang Guarantee. On-Chain Address Status As of December 15, 2025, the annual turnover of Huiwang Guarantee's pledged addresses was 1.995 billion USDT. As shown in the chart below, affected by the Telegram ban in May 2025, Huiwang Guarantee's capital turnover declined significantly and continued to shrink. In 2025, Huiwang Guarantee had over 400,000 active on-chain addresses, while Tudou Guarantee had over 120,000 active addresses. In December, due to the operational issues of Huiwang Payment, the inflow of funds to the pledged addresses decreased significantly. Huiwang Payment had previously used pledged addresses of Tudou Guarantee for fund laundering, further confirming the connection between Huiwang and Tudou Guarantee. Referring to the chart below, since Huiwang Guarantee was blocked in May 2025, the inflow of funds to pledged addresses of Tudou Guarantee has increased significantly. src="https://mmbiz.qpic.cn/mmbiz_png/DotkESTaoicE6mSD4FaKcaYg4mBsoR9ic5XykN5eelqWB3w2fJe96cgJJvIQBricl4iaV1nSO9ePzwL p5mp9TnPzQdicLIjIUdXLYRg4YIyKR8ao/640?wx_fmt=png&from=appmsg&tp=wxpic&wxfrom=5&wx_lazy=1#imgIndex=1" _="" alt="image" data-before-load-time="1771043955256" data-report-img-idx="1" data-fail="0">

2.2 Analysis of Virtual Asset Money Laundering Methods

Virtual assets, as the result of the deep integration of blockchain technology and financial innovation, play a positive role in improving the efficiency of the financial system, activating market innovation, and strengthening inclusive financial services. However, it cannot be ignored that their inherent anonymity, convenient cross-border circulation, and decentralization also provide new breeding grounds for illegal activities such as money laundering and illegal fundraising, posing a serious challenge to judicial practice. Currently, the number of virtual asset money laundering crimes and the amount of money involved are both on the rise, with increasingly sophisticated methods, directly threatening the order and security of the financial market.

2.2.1 Stablecoin Money Laundering Currently, there is a significant trend of multi-currency diversification of stablecoins, and the money laundering scenarios for different stablecoins vary considerably. The following are representative stablecoin cases from this year: ● USDT Remains a "Basic Money Laundering Vehicle" USDT remains the preferred choice for money laundering crimes. In the "Xinkangjia Financial Pyramid Scheme Case" exposed in July 2025, the platform used stablecoins as a payment method and transferred 1.8 billion USDT to a shell company in the Cayman Islands via a mixer 48 hours before its collapse. The whereabouts of a large number of investors' funds are unknown. USDT on the Tron network (TRC20-USDT) has become the preferred choice for small-amount, high-frequency transfers in black and gray market activities such as telecommunications fraud due to its high throughput and low transaction fees. ● USDC as a “Cross-Border Transit Channel” The use of USDC in money laundering following hacking attacks has increased. After the Cetus Protocol was hacked in May 2025, 3.27 million USDC were quickly exchanged for 1089 ETH via cross-chain protocols, with an average cross-chain operation time of only 17 minutes. ● DAI as a “Tool to Avoid Freezing” DAI, with its decentralized nature, has become a stablecoin option for evading freezing sanctions. Because there is no single issuer, freezing assets involved in such cases is extremely difficult. Of the $840 million in DeFi assets stolen by the North Korean Lazarus hacking group this year, over 30% were laundered by converting USDC to DAI through the MakerDAO ecosystem.

● Niche cryptocurrencies like A7 and A5 become "exclusive tools for circumventing sanctions"

This cryptocurrency is backed by Russian institutions and specifically provides cross-border payment services for sanctioned entities. Since its launch in January 2025, it has transferred a total of $70.8 billion.

2.2.2 Money Laundering with Privacy/Coin Mixing Tools

Cross-chain bridges, coin mixers, and privacy chains (such as Monero and Zcash) are playing an increasingly important role in virtual asset money laundering. Hackers, fraud groups, and other organizations use these tools to achieve rapid fund transfers, obfuscate transaction paths, and increase the difficulty for law enforcement agencies to trace the source.

2.2.3 Money Laundering Through Online Gambling Platforms Unlicensed gambling platforms utilize virtual assets to achieve rapid and anonymous fund settlements. Online gambling platforms (especially illegal platforms operating across borders) are "heavy users" of virtual assets. These platforms typically do not accept direct deposits of fiat currency, but instead require users to exchange fiat currency for stablecoins or mainstream virtual assets before transferring them to a designated address on the platform. Settlement and withdrawal of gambling funds are all completed using virtual assets. These funds may be exchanged between cryptocurrencies, transferred across borders, or withdrawn back to fiat currency, ultimately integrating into the legitimate economic system. This operation not only conceals the source of criminal proceeds but also increases the difficulty for law enforcement agencies to track and combat them, effectively forming a complete black market supply chain from gambling to money laundering.

Conclusion

The virtual asset industry in 2025 is at a critical stage where regulatory systematization, criminal specialization, and technological innovation are deeply intertwined. The shift in global regulation from vague exploration to the formation of rules has not only cleared legal obstacles for traditional financial institutions to enter the virtual asset market, but also made the market boundary between compliance and illegality increasingly clear, promoting the steady development of the virtual asset industry towards standardization and transparency. The transnational and industrial upgrading of virtual asset crimes has made it difficult for single-region regulation and law enforcement to cope with the complex criminal situation. Global regulatory cooperation and law enforcement assistance have become the trend in anti-money laundering work.

Under this trend, Beosin has combined AI and big data models to develop an advanced blockchain data intelligent analysis platform, establishing a complete regulatory technology system for the Web3 ecosystem, encompassing "smart contract security - anti-money laundering compliance - virtual asset crime prevention and governance."

Currently, Beosin provides code security auditing services to over 5,000 Web3 companies worldwide, anti-money laundering compliance technology services to over 200 financial institutions, and collaborates with regulatory and law enforcement agencies in more than 20 countries and regions globally. Feel free to contact us via the message box on our official WeChat account.

data.title: Gu Ailing's joining Benchmark is a hoax; Apollo will continue to acquire MORPHO.
data.description:
Headline

▌Benchmark Partner Clarifies: "Gu Ailing Joins" is Fake News, Just a Joke

Benchmark partner Bill Gurley issued a clarification on the X platform regarding "Gu Ailing joining," stating: "Sorry, I was just joking, this is fake news."

▌Asset Management Company Apollo to Purchase Up to 90 Million MORPHO Tokens in 4 Years

According to @bwenews, Apollo, an asset management company with $900 billion in assets under management, has reached an agreement to purchase up to 90 million MORPHO tokens within 4 years and will cooperate on on-chain lending business.

As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $66,267.10, a 24-hour change of -1.0%; ETH price is $1,944.58, a 24-hour change of +0.5%; BNB price is $613.33, a 24-hour change of +1.3%; SOL price is $78.20, a 24-hour change of -1.1%; DOGE price is $0.09312, a 24-hour change of +2.8%; XRP price is $1.36, a 24-hour change of +0.1%. The price of TRX is $0.2796, up 0.8% in the last 24 hours; the price of WLFI is $0.1072, up 4.5% in the last 24 hours; and the price of HYPE is $30.75, up 4.9% in the last 24 hours.

Policy

▌Trump advisor Epshteyn and others face class-action lawsuit from investors for promoting the Meme coin "Let's Go Brandon"

Trump advisor Epshteyn and others are facing a class-action lawsuit from investors for promoting the Meme coin "Let's Go Brandon." The lawsuit, filed in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia, accuses individuals of knowingly making false claims about the coin's decentralization, while in reality, the operators had control over smart contracts, governance, and transactions, including the power to freeze accounts.

Policy

▌Trump advisor Epshteyn and others face class-action lawsuit from investors for promoting the Meme coin "Let's Go Brandon."

▌US Treasury Secretary Bessent: Prospects for passing the CLARITY Act will be completely dashed if Democrats control the House after the midterm elections

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent stated that pushing through the CLARITY Act could help improve market sentiment amid the current persistent market downturn. In an interview with CNBC on Friday, Bessent said that the stalled progress of the CLARITY Act due to concerns from crypto industry executives has negatively impacted the industry.

... He stated, "As we go through this historic sell-off, I think a clear direction on the CLARITY bill would give the market tremendous confidence, and we can move forward from there." However, Bessent added, "If the Democrats win the House, which is definitely not what I'm hoping for, then the prospect of a deal would be completely shattered." Bessent stated that given the potential power shift in the 2026 midterm elections, it is crucial to push the bill through "as soon as possible" and send it to President Trump for his signature before the US spring (late March to late June). According to data from the US House of Representatives, Republicans currently hold a narrow majority of 218 seats to 214. On Polymarket, 47% of traders believe that the 2026 midterm elections will result in a "divided Congress," with each party controlling one house. The Dutch House of Representatives passed a legislative proposal on February 13th to impose a 36% capital gains tax on savings and most liquid investments, including cryptocurrencies. The proposal passed with 93 votes, meeting the 75-vote threshold for passage. According to the proposal, gains from savings accounts, cryptocurrencies, most equity investments, and interest-bearing financial instruments would be taxable regardless of whether the assets are sold. Certain assets, such as startup equity and non-investment physical assets, would be exempt. The proposal still needs approval from the Dutch Senate to take effect, and if passed, it will be implemented in the 2028 tax year. Opponents argue that the bill will encourage capital flows to jurisdictions with more favorable tax policies. Investor calculations show that an investor who invests €1,000 per month for 40 years will see their final return drop from €3.32 million to €1.885 million under a 36% tax rate, a difference of €1.435 million.

Blockchain Applications

▌Tether Strategically Invests in Hyperliquid Frontend Dreamcash, Launching Perpetual Contracts for TSLA, Gold, etc.

Stablecoin giant Tether announced a strategic investment in Hyperliquid's mobile interface Dreamcash, and the two companies jointly launched the RWA perpetual contract market using USDT0 as collateral. The first batch of 10 perpetual contracts covers commodities such as the S&P 500 index, gold, and silver, as well as US stocks such as Tesla, Nvidia, Google, Amazon, and Meta. Selini Capital serves as the liquidity provider. These markets are built on Hyperliquid's HIP-3 standard, support custom collateral types, and allow third-party front-ends to autonomously deploy markets after staking 500,000 HYPE tokens. Dreamcash stated that Tether will support its $200,000 weekly incentive program, rewarding contributors to USDT trading volume. Dreamcash is a non-custodial mobile application developed by Supreme Liquid Labs, designed specifically for Hyperliquid trading.

Cryptocurrency

▌F2Pool Co-founder: There are fundamental differences in economics between space Bitcoin mining and space AI data centers

F2Pool co-founder Wang Chun (@satofishi) stated in an article on the X platform that there are fundamental differences in economics between space Bitcoin mining and space AI data centers. For space AI data centers, the potential for scalability is almost limitless.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌What are long and short positions?

Long and short positions represent opposing strategies used by investors and traders to anticipate price movements of an asset under consideration. In the cryptocurrency space, going long and short still apply to concepts from traditional financial markets. To profit from a rise in cryptocurrency prices, going long means buying it and expecting its value to increase over time. In contrast, going short in the cryptocurrency market means selling a cryptocurrency that you don't actually own in anticipation of a price drop, then buying it back at a cheaper cost to close the position and profit from the price decline.

Cryptocurrency traders and investors utilize these strategies to navigate the high volatility and speculative nature of digital assets and capitalize on opportunities in both bullish and bearish market conditions. In cryptocurrency trading, a long position begins by buying an asset in the hope that its price will rise, while a short position begins by disposing of an asset (often borrowed) in the hope that its price will fall. Closing a position means buying the asset at a lower price to gain profit, while exiting a long position means selling the asset at a higher price to lock in profits. Entry and exit points are crucial for the successful implementation of these strategies. Cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate substantial profits through price appreciation, but they also come with significant risks of market volatility and potential losses. Despite these risks, cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate significant returns. The opportunity to profit from price increases is the primary benefit. Short cryptocurrency positions can be rewarding by betting on price declines, but they also carry significant risks due to market volatility, unlimited potential losses, and unpredictable price increases. Shorting cryptocurrencies requires precise timing, meticulous risk management, and continuous market monitoring to successfully navigate inherent volatility and maximize potential gains while limiting losses.

data.title: Golden Web3.0 Daily Report | Coinbase's Q4 Results Miss Expectations
data.description:
DeFi Data

1. Total Market Cap of DeFi Tokens: $48.178 Billion

Total Market Cap of DeFi

Data source: coingecko

2. Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours: $39.99

2.24-hour NFT transaction volume: $1.2186 billionUSD

NFT Total Market Capitalization, Top Ten Projects by Market Capitalization Data Source: Coinmarketcap

Headline

Coinbase's Q4 Results Miss Expectations, Transaction Revenue Falls Below $1 Billion

According to financial reports, Coinbase's Q4 results missed expectations, with total revenue of $1.78 billion (estimated at $1.83 billion) and adjusted EPS of $0.66 (estimated at $0.86).

Trading revenue plummeted to $983 million, a double decline both sequentially and year-over-year, falling below the billion-dollar mark for the first time since 2023. Subscription revenue was $727 million, also down sequentially. As of February 10th, the company's trading revenue was approximately $420 million. First-quarter subscription revenue is projected to be between $550 million and $630 million. Coinbase's stock price rebounded slightly in after-hours trading, but is still down 40% year-to-date. Coinbase stated, "We remain optimistic about the long-term prospects of the cryptocurrency industry. The cryptocurrency market is cyclical, and experience tells us that its actual performance is never as good or as bad as it appears. While asset prices may fluctuate, the undercurrent of technological change and cryptocurrency product applications continues."

MEME Hot Topics

CZ: Meme Coins Must Have Strong Cultural Attributes for Long-Term Development

On February 12th, Binance founder CZ stated in an AMA: I'm not a very good Meme trader.

I don't know what strategies the truly profitable Meme traders use. However, if you buy every Meme coin that someone else is trading based on my tweets, I can guarantee you'll lose money. I think that's pretty much certain. People might take this and twist it in the wrong direction. But what I'm trying to say is: if you're a Meme trader, you have to develop your own strategy. I don't know what the right strategy is either. But I believe that for Meme coins to last, they need a very strong cultural element. Look at those that have survived for a long time, like Dogecoin and Floki; they've been around for so long because they have a very loyal following. The newer ones are riskier. So my advice is: if you're unsure what you're doing, basically, don't touch it. Unless you really understand it, and I'm not an expert in this area.

                  DeFi Hot Topics

                  Forward Industries Financial Report: Holding Approximately 6.962 Million SOL and Earning Over 110,000 SOL Staking Rewards at the End of 2025

                  According to Jinse Finance, Nasdaq-listed Solana treasury company Forward Industries released its latest financial report, which indicates that as of December 31, 2025, it held a total of 6,962,501 SOL, and generated 112,171 SOL staking rewards through staking. It also held approximately $25.4 million in cash and had no institutional debt.

                  Jinse Finance reports that Nasdaq-listed Solana treasury company Forward Industries released its latest financial report, which indicates that as of December 31, 2025, it held a total of 6,962,501 SOL, and generated 112,171 SOL staking rewards through staking. It also held approximately $25.4 million in cash and had no institutional debt.

                  However, due to the overall decline in the crypto market, Forward Industries disclosed that its Q1 FY2026 results showed a loss of $560.2 million in digital assets and a impairment charge of $33 million in digital assets. Binance will support the MANTRA (OM) token swap and rebranding. According to Jinse Finance, Binance will support the MANTRA (OM) token swap, incremental upgrade, and rebranding to MANTRA (OM). Therefore, Binance will cease trading and remove all existing OM spot trading pairs (OM/USDT, OM/USDC, and OM/TRY) at 11:00 AM (UTC+8) on March 2, 2026, and will automatically cancel all pending orders. Binance will reopen MANTRA/USDT, MANTRA/USDC, and MANTRA/TRY spot trading at 4:00 PM (UTC+8) on March 4, 2026. Binance will suspend deposits and withdrawals of old OM tokens on March 2, 2026 at 11:30 AM (UTC+8). Note: Old OM tokens deposited after this time will not be credited to your account; please deposit in advance. Circle Mints $500 Million USDC on the Solana Network According to Jinse Finance, market sources indicate that Circle has minted $500 million (500,000,000) USDC stablecoins on the Solana network. Polymarket Extends Fee Mechanism to Sports Market On February 13, Rodrigo, an official from Polymarket, posted on Discord that they will extend their fee mechanism to the NCAA (College Basketball) and Serie A (Italian Football League) markets. The fee structure is the same as the "15-minute cryptocurrency price fluctuation market," where the order taker pays the fee and the order placer receives a rebate. However, the coefficients are different—the fee rate is 0.0175, the index is 1, and the maker rebate is 25%, resulting in lower overall costs. Starting at midnight UTC on Wednesday, February 18, 2026, all new NCAAB and Serie A events created after that time will be subject to the new fee mechanism. Existing events will not be affected. The first rebate will be issued at midnight UTC on February 19. Ethereum weekly transaction volume reaches 17.3 million, a record high. According to Jinse Finance, market news indicates that Ethereum's weekly transaction volume reached 17.3 million, a record high; meanwhile, the median fee has dropped to a record low. Token Terminal data shows that Ethereum's scaling upgrade has been effective. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Musk's new "pie in the sky": a lunar base
data.description:
Author: Ye Huiwen, Wall Street Insights

Elon Musk is building a grand narrative for his newly merged business empire, blending space exploration and artificial intelligence. He is attempting to replace his long-held but unprofitable Mars colonization plan with the vision of a "Lunar Base Alpha," using it as a new attraction for talent and capital.

According to TechCrunch, during the merger of SpaceX and xAI and the subsequent restructuring, Musk presented this new blueprint to employees: establishing a city on the moon and using lunar mass-driven systems to launch AI satellites into deep space. This idea was proposed after xAI experienced a wave of executive departures, and Musk is attempting to use it as a new recruitment strategy to boost market expectations for the potential IPO of the merged company. He stated that to achieve a computing power target exceeding one terawatt per year, it is necessary to go to the moon and utilize solar energy.

This strategic shift comes at a time when SpaceX publicly abandoned its long-held goal of colonizing Mars. Rather than pursuing a commercially viable Mars program, Musk now favors a science fiction metaphor based on the "Kardashev scale"—capturing solar energy to power large-scale AI models by building extraterrestrial infrastructure. Musk claims the company could use "a few percent of solar energy" to train and run AI, a vision designed to provide the merged entity with a unique value proposition differentiating it from other AI labs. For investors, while highly speculative, this vision logically attempts to combine SpaceX's launch capabilities with the computational demands of xAI. Despite the significant technological and cost barriers to mass-producing supercomputers on the moon, this narrative could potentially fuel retail investor enthusiasm, attempting to propel SpaceX stock to the next Tesla, while simultaneously offering new technical challenges to engineers weary of traditional large language model development. Strategic Focus Shift: From Mars to Lunar AI Center Musk's new vision was revealed at the end of his presentation at the xAI all-hands conference, a segment where he typically showcases renderings of a Mars landing and discusses the future of multi-planetary humanity. However, this time the focus shifted to a lunar base. After outlining plans to establish an AI data center in Earth orbit, Musk further proposed that to overcome computing power bottlenecks, a move into deep space is necessary. He envisioned using a large magnetic levitation train (i.e., a mass drive) to project a lunar-built space computer into the solar system. This shift didn't come out of nowhere. TechCrunch points out that SpaceX had previously publicly withdrawn from its Mars colonization goals. Back in 2017, SpaceX abandoned its plan to convert the Dragon spacecraft into a Mars lander due to technical challenges and high costs. Its core launch vehicle, Starship, has also been scaled back to focus on two more profitable short-term missions: launching Starlink communications satellites and executing NASA's $4 billion manned lunar landing contract. In contrast, the Mars program lacks a clear payer, while the lunar base serves more closely the current logic of expanding AI computing power. Re-engineering Recruiting and Narrative Tools For the past nine years, Mars colonization has been an extremely effective recruitment tool and brand moat for SpaceX. The "Conquer Mars" slogan not only united internal employees but also set SpaceX apart from many contractors content with government contracts. Now, with xAI incorporated into the corporate landscape, Musk needs a new narrative to integrate rocket manufacturing with artificial intelligence. A former executive once complained that "all AI labs are doing the exact same thing, which is boring," and the idea of ​​"mass-producing solar system-level supercomputers on the moon" clearly breaks this homogenization. Musk's citation of the Kardashev scale—a theory measuring a civilization's energy utilization level—provides a theoretical framework for this vision. He argues that early civilizations utilize planetary resources, while advanced civilizations build space infrastructure to capture stellar energy. This grand science fiction narrative aims to reignite engineers' passion and imbue xAI with a sense of mission beyond that of a traditional software company. Business Logic and Market Reaction While the idea of ​​building a lunar base seems distant, there is a certain business logic behind it. Experts point out that with the surge in demand and rising costs for data centers on Earth, building data centers in Earth orbit may be feasible in the 2030s. SpaceX's acquisition of xAI and its use of space assets to build data centers may be a bet on the spatialization of future computing infrastructure. However, establishing a "self-sufficient city" on the moon to produce tons of advanced computers hinges on a significant reduction in the cost of accessing space and a solution to the problem of transporting raw materials. This vision is currently viewed more as an "extended goal." For the market, if this narrative resonates with retail investors, it could drive up the valuation of the merged company. As TechCrunch analyzed, this is an attempt to transform SpaceX stock into the next market hotspot, while also providing a unique differentiator for an AI company that might otherwise be known only for its large language models.

data.title: Before buying at the bottom, understand the two types of market pullbacks.
data.description:
Author: Todd Wenning; Translator: Deep Tide TechFlow

Academic financial theory categorizes risk into systematic risk and idiosyncratic risk. Similarly, stock drawdowns are divided into two types: market-driven systematic drawdowns (such as the 2008 financial crisis) and company-specific idiosyncratic drawdowns (such as the current software stock crash caused by AI concerns).

Todd Wenning uses FactSet as an example, pointing out that during systematic drawdowns, you can leverage behavioral advantages (patiently waiting for the market to recover); but during idiosyncratic drawdowns, you need analytical advantages—a more accurate vision of the company ten years from now than the market.

In the current context of AI impacting software stocks, investors must distinguish: is this a temporary market panic, or is the moat truly collapsing?

Don't use blunt behavioral solutions to solve problems that require nuanced analysis.

The full text is as follows: Academic financial theory posits two types of risk: systematic and idiosyncratic. Systematic risk is unavoidable market risk. It cannot be eliminated through diversification, and it is the only type of risk from which you can earn a return. On the other hand, idiosyncratic risk is company-specific risk. Because you can cheaply buy diversified portfolios of unrelated businesses, you do not earn a return for taking on this type of risk. We can discuss modern portfolio theory another day, but the systematic-idiosyncratic framework is helpful for understanding different types of drawdowns (the percentage decline from the peak to the trough of an investment) and how we, as investors, should assess opportunities. From the moment we picked up our first book on value investing, we were taught to take advantage of Mr. Market's frustration during stock sell-offs. If we remain calm when he loses his temper, we will prove ourselves to be steadfast value investors. But not all pullbacks are the same. Some are market-driven (systematic), while others are company-specific (trait-based). Before you act, you need to know which type you're looking at. The recent sell-off in software stocks due to AI concerns illustrates this point. Let's look at the 20-year drawdown history between FactSet (FDS, blue) and the S&P 500 (measured by the SPY ETF, orange).

Source: Koyfin, as of February 12, 2026

FactSet's drawdowns during the financial crisis were primarily systemic. In 2008/09, the entire market was worried about the durability of the financial system, and FactSet was not immune to these concerns, especially since it sells products to financial professionals.

... At that time, the stock pullback had little to do with FactSet's economic moat, but rather with whether FactSet's moat would be important should the financial system collapse. The 2025/26 FactSet pullback was the opposite. Here, concerns were almost entirely focused on FactSet's moat and growth potential, and the widespread worry about accelerating AI capabilities disrupting pricing power in the software industry. During systemic pullbacks, you can more rationally make time arbitrage bets. History shows that markets tend to rebound, and companies with strong moats may even be stronger than before, so if you are willing and able to remain patient when others are panicking, you can leverage a strong appetite to take advantage of behavioral strengths.

Photo courtesy of Walker Fenton on Unsplash

However, in a traited pullback, the market tells you that there's something wrong with the business itself. In particular, it suggests that the business's terminal value is becoming increasingly uncertain.

Therefore, if you want to take advantage of traited pullbacks, you need to have an analytical advantage in addition to a behavioral advantage.

To succeed, you need to have a more accurate vision of what the company will look like ten years from now than what the market price currently implies.

Even if you know a company very well, this is not easy to do.

Stocks don't typically fall 50% relative to the market for no reason. This happens when many previously stable holders—even some you might respect for their thorough research—have to give in. If you're going to step in as a buyer during a traited pullback, you need an answer to why these previously well-informed and thoughtful investors were wrong to sell, and why your vision is correct. There's a fine line between conviction and arrogance. Whether you're holding a stock in a pullback or looking to start a new position, it's important to understand what kind of bet you're making. Traited pullbacks can tempt value investors to look for opportunities. Before you take the plunge, make sure you're not using a blunt, behavioral solution to a problem that requires nuanced analysis. Be patient, stay focused.

data.title: Will Clawnch be the breakthrough point for AI+Crypto in 2026?
data.description:
Among the crypto projects spurred by the recent Moltbook craze, @Clawnch_Bot has garnered significant attention. This is primarily due to the fact that many influential figures within the ecosystem followed it from the outset, with even Coinbase's Jesse frequently sharing updates about the project online. Initially, I only saw it as a launch platform. Although it was touted as a launch platform specifically prepared for AI Agents and received various endorsements, its function and purpose seemed similar to Pumpfun—still just a large casino for meme coins. Therefore, I remained indifferent, simply observing and tracking it like any other project. However, recently, an online post analyzed the mechanism and characteristics of this project in detail (see the reference link at the end of the article for details), which showed me some important differences between this project and Pumpfun. Today, I'll share the viewpoints from that article with you. The author first summarizes the mechanism of Pumpfun, pointing out that it is essentially a giant human casino. In that casino, a zero-sum game is played; for every winner, someone else loses. The entire mechanism lacks any economic loop and is utterly unsustainable. Next, the author lists several key features of Clawnch: - AI Agent text="">ClawnchShanghai coins,80% shall be attributed in the form ofWETHAI Agent

- ClawnchAlso integratedMOLTEN, aAI Agentscollaborative token issuance, with revenue split according to each party's expertise and contribution in a 4:3:3 ratio. Here, AI Agents collaborate and generate business models. ...

- except style="font-family:宋体">Hats Protocol + Safe Multisigetc. In short, Clawnch is not only a platform for launching tokens but also a platform for integrating skills. AI Agents that issue tokens on it can earn money by issuing tokens and continuously learn new skills. Skills (leaf="">skills). Once they earn money, they will use these funds to maintain their "survival," "purchase" their own needs, "cooperate" with each other, and construct business models. This economic form and business model has far surpassed Pumpfun. Despite this, among these characteristics, there is one that I believe deserves further observation and consideration. The AI ​​Agents' initial revenue comes from transaction fees. This is the primary driving force enabling the entire system to operate. But why would users trade these tokens, for example, buy them? I think this initial action is still mainly driven by emotion. At least at this stage, I don't seem to see many AI Agents with mature and profitable business models, and then using that as a selling point to issue tokens on Clawnch. So in this respect, it still retains some of the characteristics of meme coins. But unlike Pumpfun, which is simply a casino with no other features, it is still a very basic casino. It also provides skills, collaboration, and "employment." Therefore, tokens on Pumpfun may never escape the vicious cycle of meme coins. However, it's possible that Clawnch could indeed create AI Agents that leverage the skills, collaborative environment, and "job opportunities" provided by the platform to build their own business models and even empower their tokens, ultimately breaking free from the vicious cycle of meme coins. Of course, the above logic is purely theoretical. Whether theoretical derivations can be put into practice depends on solid data, namely, whether the economic scale of the system can grow rapidly.

Existing data shows thatAI AgentsAlready earned onClawnch130$ million, but that's still too small. In contrast, Pumpfunhas earned $700 million to date$700 million.$ million. ...Calibri">$ million.$ million. Therefore, at this stage, it is still just an experiment and exploration that needs to be verified, and no recommendations or investment advice are given. When can you earn more fees than Pumpfun? If so, it might actually indicate that the above logic is feasible and that AI Agents can indeed form a sustainable and healthy economic entity; otherwise, it is probably just another pioneering experiment in the exploration of the path of AI + Crypto. In 2026, we hope to see truly groundbreaking and disruptive breakthroughs in the AI ​​+ Crypto field.

data.title: The European Central Bank (ECB) has released a policy targeting unlicensed crypto service providers.
data.description: The European Banking Authority (EBA) issued an opinion on the 12th. Based on the revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2), the transition period set forth in the EBA's No-Action Letter of June 2, 2025 (EBA/Op/2025/08) ends on March 2, 2026. The EBA has made recommendations to National Competent Authorities (NCAs) regarding how to proceed after the end of the transition period. The recommendation is to allow Crypto Asset Service Providers (CASPs) to continue trading eligible electronic money tokens (EMTs) for nine months after submitting and awaiting PSD2 authorization applications. The opinion summarizes that it recommends NCAs continue to allow CASPs to offer eligible electronic money tokens (EMTs) after March 2, 2026, even if they do not yet hold a PSD2 license. It also recommends that NCAs require CASPs that do not meet all these conditions to cease offering such EMT services. If necessary, it is recommended that NCAs cooperate with the Crypto Asset Market Regulation (MiCA) and/or other national law enforcement agencies to ensure compliance. This opinion follows the EBA's letter of non-action issued on June 2, 2025, in response to the European Commission's request for clarification regarding the interaction between EMT transaction CASPs eligible for payment services under PSD2 and MiCA. This letter of non-action allows CASPs to continue providing these services and requires a second authorization under PSD2, but not immediately, with a nine-month transition period. Furthermore, the letter recommends that national authorities only consider crypto asset services with EMT as payment services as part of such authorization, thereby reducing the number of CASPs requiring such authorization. The report also recommends that national authorities adopt simplified authorization processes, fully utilizing the information already provided during the MICA authorization process to reduce the administrative burden on CASPs. Since the letter of no action was issued, more than 100 CASPs have informally submitted or submitted applications for authorization as payment service providers (PSPs) to national authorities. With the transition period nearing its end, and given the potential differences in the number of applications across member states, the EBA is issuing this opinion to advise NCAs on how to prioritize their authorization efforts after the transition period ends. Legal Basis: The EBA’s authority to issue this opinion is based on Article 29(1)(a) of Regulation 1093/2010[1], which requires the EBA to actively participate in building a unified regulatory culture and consistent regulatory practices within the Union, and to ensure uniformity of procedures and methods within the Union, including issuing opinions to national authorities.

data.title: UK on-chain government bonds and Thai crypto derivatives: Global sovereigns embracing digital assets
data.description:
The UK government recently appointed HSBC's Orion platform to pilot the issuance of digital gilt-edged bonds (DIGIT), marking the official entry of the sovereign debt market into the blockchain era. Meanwhile, Thailand approved digital assets as derivatives and underlying assets in its capital markets, becoming a new benchmark for crypto trading by Southeast Asian institutions. These two initiatives, though located in the UK and Southeast Asia respectively, together outline the accelerating trend of global sovereign institutions embracing blockchain.

UK: Digital Phnom Penh Bond Pilot

On February 11, the UK Treasury announced that it has designated Orion, HSBC's digital asset platform, as the official pilot issuer for digital government bonds (Phnom Penh bonds). This is another significant development in the sovereign debt sector following the Bank of England's digital pound test in 2024.

Pilot Core: The Orion platform will be responsible for the issuance, settlement, and secondary market circulation of DIGIT (Digital Gilt Instrument Token), using Ethereum or permissioned blockchain technology to achieve T+0 real-time settlement and 24/7 trading. Target Scenarios: Fragmented bondholders can achieve instant splitting and reinvestment through tokenization; institutional investors can embed ESG terms or automated interest payments in the form of smart contracts, improving liquidity and transparency. Regulatory Endorsement: The UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has pre-issued guidelines for a regulatory sandbox of tokenized assets to ensure the pilot program complies with the capital adequacy and custody requirements of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023. HSBC Europe CEO Georges Elhedery stated: "Digital gilt-edged bonds are not just a technological upgrade, but a global paradigm shift in the sovereign debt market." This pilot program directly responds to the UK's Technology Future Strategy, aiming to reduce government financing costs and enhance international attractiveness through blockchain technology. Thailand: Embracing Crypto Derivatives Thousands of miles away, Thailand's Ministry of Finance approved a proposal this Tuesday to formally include digital assets in the list of underlying assets for derivatives and capital markets, making it the first ASEAN jurisdiction to embrace crypto derivatives through national legislation. **Policy Highlights:** Bitcoin, Ethereum, and other mainstream cryptocurrencies and stablecoins can be used as underlying assets or collateral for futures, options, and swap contracts. The Thai Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) will simultaneously issue guidelines on leverage ratios, risk reserves, and investor suitability. **Regional Positioning:** Thailand aims to become an "ASEAN crypto derivatives hub," attracting global institutions to participate in Thai baht stablecoin/BTC/ETH perpetual contract trading through its clearinghouse, directly competing with Singapore and Hong Kong. Infrastructure Support: The Thai Digital Asset Exchange (DAX) will be upgraded to a licensed derivatives platform, introducing an on-chain margin system and cross-chain collateralization, supporting USDT/THB hedging products. Thai Finance Minister Pichai Chunhavajira emphasized that this move aims to "revitalize the traditional derivatives market through crypto assets, while strengthening anti-money laundering and market manipulation prevention." This policy directly echoes Thailand's "Digital Asset Ecosystem Roadmap 2025-2030," with the goal of increasing derivatives trading volume from the current $15 billion per year to the $50 billion level. Extended Impact: The Global Sovereign Blockchain Race Intensifies The actions of the UK and Thailand may seem different, but they point to the same strategic logic: traditional sovereign debt and derivatives markets are limited by T+2 settlement, tiered clearing, and geographical restrictions, while blockchain brings real-time performance and global programmability, directly reducing financing and transaction costs. As the US CLARITY Act and the EU MiCA framework become clearer, sovereign states, fearing marginalization, are eager to attract global asset management giants (such as BlackRock and Fidelity) through "national-level digital asset products." These two developments will trigger a domino effect—Hong Kong/Singapore's counterattack: Following the issuance of the first batch of stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong in March, on-chain government bond issuance is expected to proceed simultaneously; the Monetary Authority of Singapore may accelerate the tokenization testing of Project Guardian's derivatives. Pressure from the US and Europe: The UK's DIGIT pilot program will likely spur the US Senate to expedite the GENIUS stablecoin bill; the EU may respond with a combination of a digital euro and sovereign debt tokenization. Emerging Market Opportunities: Brazil, the UAE, India, and other countries may replicate the Thai model, attracting foreign investment through crypto derivatives to achieve limited opening of their capital accounts. From HSBC Orion's on-chain gilt-edged bonds to the Thai SEC's pilot program for BTC perpetual contracts, global sovereign institutions are collectively validating an assumption: blockchain is not only an efficiency tool for private markets, but also the future infrastructure for national credit and monetary policy. When the UK and Thailand launch their "digital sovereign assets," the flow of global asset management funds will be reshaped—the traditional bond and derivatives markets may be facing their final on-chain reshuffle. This article is for reference only and does not constitute any investment advice. The market is risky; invest with caution.

data.title: Understand the economy like you understand an ecosystem
data.description:
The ending of the movie *The Big Short* coincides with the start of the 2008 financial crisis. Aside from a few astute investment geniuses in the film, most people lost everything when the bubble burst. Have you ever pondered this question: Why is mainstream economics so often unable to predict financial crises? Why did the 2008 crash catch all economists off guard? Ethan Buckman was once a biophysicist. When he turned to studying economic systems, he discovered an absurd fact—the core assumption of mainstream economics is "equilibrium." In traditional economic models, experts attempt to bring the system to equilibrium through complex equations, as if the economy were a static machine. But according to the second law of thermodynamics, everything in the universe tends towards chaos, and life exists precisely because of thermal imbalances. This podcast episode, the fourth in the Green Pill series "Ethereum Localism," focuses on alternative economics. Hosts Alex, Max, and Josh invite three deep thinkers to re-examine our fundamental understanding of economics, money, and wealth from the perspectives of biology, anthropology, and community practice. The three guests reach a consensus from different angles: we need to understand the economy like we understand an ecosystem—it's not a machine, but an organism; not static, but fluid; not about possession, but about circulation. Guests: Ethan Buckman. As co-founder of cross-chain giant Cosmos and CEO of Informal Systems, Ethan is not only dedicated to technology development but also deeply involved in software research and education. With a strong background in biophysics, Ethan tends to analyze economic issues from the perspective of complex systems theory and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics. Currently, he is focusing on the development of the Cycles protocol, attempting to solve the debt liquidation problem in the economy through graph theory, and promoting more liquid and sustainable monetary experiments. Julio Linarez is an economic anthropologist from Guatemala, now residing in Berlin. He studied money and value theory at the London School of Economics under the late anthropologist David Graeber and conducted fieldwork on monetary flows in impoverished communities in Bangladesh. Julio is also one of the initiators of the Circles UBI (Universal Basic Income) project, which attempts to self-organize a basic income system without relying on the government. Julio himself is dedicated to exploring anti-colonial financial systems, advocating the abolition of the existing debt-based dollar hegemony, and the establishment of a new economic structure based on community self-governance, direct democracy, and ecological justice. Alana Kanei is a board member of Hours Exchange. Hours Exchange is a community currency project located in Corvallis, Oregon. They emphasize that unlike anonymous transactions involving the US dollar, local currency exchanges create a "relationship economy"—each exchange leaves a trace of interpersonal connection that weaves into a mutual aid network over time. Hours Exchange believes this enduring relationship is more valuable than any single currency or token.

Podcast Time | May 6, 2025

A Leap from Biophysics to Monetary Theory

In our everyday understanding, money and economic laws are often regarded as immutable physical laws, like "universal gravitation."
